coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-enhanced chemiluminescence method for the sensitive determination of iodide
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yue
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen doped carbon dots regulated by formaldehyde were synthesized using citric acid and melamine by a simple hydrothermal method. The obtained carbon dots (MFCD) significantly enhanced the weak chemiluminescence (CL) reaction of K2Cr2O7 with NaHSO3 by means of energy transform from the intermediate of excited sulphur dioxide (SO2*) and the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons. Interestingly, it was discovered that iodide was effective in enhancing the CL intensity of this system. Based on this finding, the MFCD-based CL system as a sensitive and selective probe was applied for detection of nM levels of iodide ions. Under optimum conditions, the probe had a linear calibration range from 0.03 to 5.0 μM with a detection limit of 5.6 nM. The developed CL method was applied for the detection of iodide in milk and mineral water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Carbon dots
Sulfite
Dichromate
Iodide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV-vis and FL spectroscopy of MFCD
Application of CL system for the determination of iodide

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (no. 2013011013–3 ), and the 1331 Engineering of Shanxi Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI-CL system. F, flow cell; PMT, photomultipier tube; HV, negative high voltage; PC, personal computer.

(a) QY of different synthesis temperatures at fixed 10 h synthesis time and different synthesis times at fixed 180 °C synthesis temperature of MFCD, (b) UV-vis spectra and FL spectra of MFCD (Inset: photographs of MFCD at visible light (1) and UV light at 365 nm (2)), (c) FL spectra at different excitation wavelengths of MFCD, and (d) pH dependent FL of MFCD.

(a) CL of the K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 with CD, MCD and MFCD, respectively, (b) CL of the K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 with MFCD at different synthesis temperatures and fixed 10 h synthesis time, (c) CL of the K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 with MFCD at different synthesis times and fixed 180 °C synthesis temperature.

(a) CL kinetic profiles of the K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system (curve 1) and MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system (curve 2–4) and MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3-KI system (curve 5). (1) injecting K2Cr2O7 into NaHSO3, (2) injecting MFCD into K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3, (3) injecting NaHSO3 into MFCD-K2Cr2O7, (4) injecting K2Cr2O7 into MFCD-NaHSO3, and (5) injecting K2Cr2O7 to MFCD-NaHSO3-KI, (b) FL spectra of MFCD and MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system, (c) CL spectra of the MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 with and without KI, (d) the logarithmic relationship between CL intensity and iodide concentration.
Comparison of FL and CL for iodide detection.
Tolerable concentration ratio of various coexistent substances on the determination of iodide.

row.entry: Pb2+, EDTA, lysine, melamine, formaldehyde, TCA
Br−, Fe2+, Fe3+, VB6, VB1, folic acid, tryptophan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . Results for the determination of iodide in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-enhanced chemiluminescence method for the sensitive determination of iodide

ce:author.ce:roles: Investigation; Methodology; Data curation; Writing - original draft
Conceptualization; Resources; Supervision; Writing - review & editing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041000, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen doped carbon dots regulated by formaldehyde were synthesized using citric acid and melamine by a simple hydrothermal method. The obtained carbon dots (MFCD) significantly enhanced the weak chemiluminescence (CL) reaction of K2Cr2O7 with NaHSO3 by means of energy transform from the intermediate of excited sulphur dioxide (SO2*) and the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons. Interestingly, it was discovered that iodide was effective in enhancing the CL intensity of this system. Based on this finding, the MFCD-based CL system as a sensitive and selective probe was applied for detection of nM levels of iodide ions. Under optimum conditions, the probe had a linear calibration range from 0.03 to 5.0 μM with a detection limit of 5.6 nM. The developed CL method was applied for the detection of iodide in milk and mineral water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is the conversion of chemical energy into the emission of visible light as a result of a chemiexcitation caused by chemical reaction. Coupled with flow injection (FI) technology, CL detection has several attractions of high sensitivity, low detection limit, wide dynamic range, rapid response, automation, and simple instrumentation, and becomes a powerful analytical method applied in various fields such as pharmaceuticals, biological fluids, foods and environmental matrices [1]. CL system composed of sulfite with various oxidants is one of the best known and most useful system. However, the CL generated from the reaction of sulphite with many oxidants (KMnO4, Ce(IV), H2O2, KBrO3, etc.) is ultra-weak due to low quantum yield (QY) of excited state SO2 molecules (SO2*). At this time, this CL reaction is limited in terms of QY, the introduction of nanomaterials can improve its QY and efficiency, and extend its applications [2,3].

Carbon dots (CD), a new type of carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterial that appeared in 2004 [4], are composed of dispersed spherical carbon particles. Owing to their extremely small particle size, good fluorescence (FL) properties, high aqueous solubility, low toxicity and cost, wide range of raw materials, simple preparation method, ease of functionalization and environmental friendly, CD have attracted much attention from researchers and have been widely used in many fields [5]. Especially, the CL of CD opens up new opportunities for their potential analytical applications [6–8]. Now, CD synthesized from various raw materials were used to increase weak CL intensity from the reactions between sulphite and bromate or hydrogen peroxide and so on [9,10]. To the best of our knowledge, CD have not yet been used for the CL reaction between K2Cr2O7 with NaHSO3.

Iodine is a necessary micronutrient element for human growth and development, and it is also known as intelligence element. Iodine deficiency will affect the normal development of children brain, resulting in irreversible intellectual disability. Although goiter is the most visible sequelae of iodine deficiency, the major impact of hypothyroidism due to iodine deficiency is impaired neurodevelopment, particularly early in life [11]. Conversely, the excessive iodine intake may cause hyperthyroidism and thyroid autoimmune diseases [12]. Iodide is mainly ingested by foods and drinking water. Hence, monitoring the iodide content in foods is significant both for physiological and healthy. Driven by the demand, many strategies have been proposed for the detection of iodide including ion chromatography [13], colorimetry [14], electrochemistry [15], FL [16–18], CL [19,20], and so on.

In this study, the carbon dots regulated by formaldehyde and melamine (labeled as MFCD) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. The study demonstrated that K2Cr2O7 could oxidize NaHSO3 to produce weak CL, and MFCD could greatly enhance the CL intensity from the reaction between K2Cr2O7 and NaHSO3 based on radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in the MFCD, and the transfer of energy from excited sulfur dioxide (SO2*) to the MFCD. Furtherly, the CL signal of the MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system was enhanced in the presence of iodide, thus allowed to rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of iodide with the simple CL method.

All purchased chemicals were analytical grade. Citric acid, melamine, formaldehyde, KI and NaHSO3 were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). K2Cr2O7 was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). A 0.01 M stock solution of K2Cr2O7 and KI was prepared by dissolving appropriate quantity of their solid in deionized water, respectively.

The FL spectra of the synthesized MFCD were obtained using a F-380 FL spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China), and UV-vis spectrum was studied by a TU-1810 PC spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The characterization of MFCD was accomplished by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100, Japan), X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Japan), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectrometry (Varian, USA), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, USA).

The measurements of CL intensity were made by means of a FI-CL analyzer, including a model IFIS-C FI sampler (Xi'an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China) and a BPCL ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China). The CL spectrum was recorded using high-energy cutoff filters (400–620 nm) on BPCL with FI mode. The Lenovo computer was used for data acquisition.

CL kinetic curves were acquired by batch experiments. In a 10 mL quartz cuvette, 1.0 mL 7.0 mM NaHSO3, 1.0 mL 1:50 (v/v) MFCD, 1.0 mL 0.4 M H2SO4 and 1.0 mL water/sample were premixed, and then 1.0 mL 0.5 mM K2Cr2O7 was injected by a syringe from upper injection port. Based on this, the flow line of FI-CL system was decided by changing the adding orders of the reagent solutions for achieving maximum CL intensity, and the schematic diagram of the FI system was shown in Fig. 1 . The solution of H2SO4, KI standard or sample and K2Cr2O7 were pumped continuously by the peristaltic pump 2 into flow cell at 2.5 mL/min. The H2SO4 was used as a carrier to carry the mixing NaHSO3 and MFCD solution driven by the peristaltic pump 1 into a 60 μL injection value. The light output from the CL reaction in the flow cell was detected by means of the BPCL luminescence analyzer. The full CL intensity vs. time curve was recorded and the peak height was used for quantitative determination.

The developed CL method was used for the determination of iodide in milk and mineral water samples, which were obtained from the local market. The mineral water sample was directly analyzed without any processing. For milk sample, 12.5 mL milk was added 2.5 mL of 10% trichloroacetic acid to precipitate the proteins. The mixture was shaken and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was further treated twice with 1.0 mL trichloroacetic acid according to the above steps, and then filtered through a 0.45 µm filtered membrane and diluted to the mark in a 25 mL volumetric flask for the real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-vis and FL spectroscopy of MFCD
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, MFCD was synthesized using citric acid, melamine and formaldehyde by hydrothermal method. As a contrast, MCD obtained using citric acid and melamine and CD prepared using citric acid were also prepared by the same method (see supplementary data). The effects of time and temperature of synthetic reaction on QY were studied. The results showed that the maximum QY were obtained for MFCD (67.1%, Fig. 2 a), MCD (30.9%) and CD (26.6%) at reaction temperature 180 °C and reaction time 12 h by using quinine sulfate (QY 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) as reference, respectively. The QY of MFCD was much higher than that of MCD and CD, which fully demonstrated the cooperative role of formaldehyde in enhancing the QY. The presence of formaldehyde not only increased the solubility of melamine in water, but also in the hydrothermal reaction process formaldehyde and melamine could react together or separately with citric acid to obtain more effective emission sites on MFCD and regulated the chemical state of nitrogen in MFCD. Therefore, the synergistic effect of formaldehyde and melamine was an important factor to achieve relatively high FLQY [21]. The UV-vis absorption spectrum and FL spectra were applied to monitor the optical properties of MFCD. Fig. 2b displayed no obvious absorption peak and only a wide absorption band from 200 nm to 400 nm, attributing to the n–π* transition of CO [22,23], and the FL excitation and emission spectra of MFCD settled in a wide wavelength range. The color of MFCD solution was transparent yellow under visible light, and was blue under UV irradiation (inset in Fig. 2b). The FL spectra (Fig. 2c) of MFCD were measured under different excitation wavelengths that range from 300 nm to 380 nm, and the maximum emission located at 425 nm upon excitation at 350 nm. The phenomenon of excitation dependent emission may be related to different surface states and sizes affecting the band gap of MFCD [24]. The influences of pH value on the FL of MFCD were studied. As shown in Fig. 2d, FL intensity gradually increased with the pH increasing from 1 to 3, then it decreased when pH > 3. The pH dependency of the FL of MFCD was possibly owning to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of MFCD [25]. Under low pH values, the MFCD was dissolved as isolated species in the aqueous, producing strong FL; at higher pH values, the decrease of FL caused by the aggregation of MFCD because of noncovalent molecular interactions, such as hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl groups [26].

As is known to all, a weak CL emission is observed from the oxidation of sulphite by some oxidants in acidic medium, which is not suitable for analytical applications. The CL emission intensity could be greatly enhanced in the presence of MFCD. During preliminary studies on the CL of oxidized sulphite in the presence of MFCD, permanganate and dichromate generated the most intense emission. A weak emission was observed from cerium(IV), bromate and periodate, no emission was seen with hydrogen peroxide (Fig. S2a). The effect on CL intensity of sulfite, bisulfite, thiosulfate were also researched, and the maximum CL intensity appeared with the addition of bisulfite (Fig. S2b). So two CL systems of MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system and MFCD-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system were selected and studied in depth. When same amount of iodide was added into the MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system and MFCD-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system, respectively, the CL intensity of the former increased markedly, instead, the CL intensity of the latter was slightly inhibited (Fig. S2a). Considering the sensitivity and selectivity, the MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system was used for the determination of iodide.

A set of parallel experiment was performed to further evaluate the CL properties of MFCD. MFCD, MCD and CD were added into K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 CL system, respectively. It could be noticed that the CL intensity of the K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system was increased about 44 times by adding MFCD, and about 10 times by MCD and CD, respectively (Fig. 3 a). It was presumed that the addition of formaldehyde could regulate the chemical state of nitrogen in the process of synthesizing MFCD, and thus greatly enhance the QY of MFCD [21], eventually lead to a remarkable increase in CL intensity for the NaHSO3-K2Cr2O7 system. Furthermore, previous reports showed that the time and temperature of quantum dots synthesis had influence on the performance of quantum dots [27,28]. Therefore, the CL intensity and QY of MFCD with different reaction temperatures (160–220 °C) and times (8–16 h) were discussed. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the CL signal caused by MFCD with reaction condition under 180 °C and 12 h was notable higher than that those under other conditions.

A static mode was used for the MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 CL reaction. There was weak, but fast CL when K2Cr2O7 solution was injected into NaHSO3 solution, which was roughly increased 50 times by MFCD in the acidic medium (Fig. 4 a). The mixing order of reagents played an important role in CL reaction and was evaluated. The highest CL intensity was achieved by the first mode of the injection of K2Cr2O7 compared to the injection of NaHSO3 or MFCD. In addition, the second mode of injection of NaHSO3 to MFCD-K2Cr2O7 was slightly smaller than that of the first one, indicating no reaction occurred between MFCD and K2Cr2O7 before the NaHSO3 was injected. However, almost no signal was given in the third mode of injecting MFCD into NaHSO3-K2Cr2O7. So in the design of flow line in FI system the first mode i.e. injecting K2Cr2O7 into MFCD-NaHSO3 was adopted. Moreover, the influence of raw material (i.e. citric acid, melamine, formaldehyde) was examined on the CL reaction, which approved that the enhancement in the CL reaction of K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 derived from the use of MFCD. And it should be noted that the CL signal was further enhanced with the addition of iodide. The CL intensity reached the maximum value rapidly, suggesting the enhancement process happened instantaneously. The above results confirmed that the proposed CL system could be used to detect iodide.

FL and CL spectra: the FL spectra of MFCD and MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system were shown in Fig. 4b. The FL intensity of MFCD decreased to some extent but its FL emission wavelength remained unchanged at 425 nm after the reaction. The phenomena showed that some MFCD was aggregated during the CL reaction. It was assumed that some strong oxidizing free radicals generated during the CL reaction of K2Cr2O7 and NaHSO3 might consume part of MFCD, thus lead to the decrease in FL intensity. The CL spectrum of MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system was recorded using high-energy cutoff filters (Fig. 4c), and the maximum wavelength appeared at 505 nm. The fact that CL spectra red-shifted compared to the most intense FL spectra may be explained by considering the difference in the emitting states of the CL and FL emission. It is assumed that like electrochemiluminescence, the CL of MFCD is mainly produced by surface-state transitions (due to the presence of surface traps with incomplete passivation) whereas the FL is more reminiscent of core state [6]. The energy band gap of surface states is smaller than that of the core states, and therefore the wavelength of CL emission is higher than that of FL [29,30]. In that case, it could be inferred that excited state MFCD (MFCD*) was responsible for the light emission in the present CL system.

Free radical reaction pathway: to identify the radical species, the radical scavengers of nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT), thiourea and NaN3 were used to specially scavenge •O2 –, •OH and 1O2, respectively. The results showed that about 83.3% and 89.9% of CL intensities were inhibited by the addition of 1.0 mM NBT and 1.0 mM thiourea, respectively. Additionally, a 1.0 mM of NaN3 could quench about 6% of CL intensity. These results evidently confirmed the generation of oxygen radicals, among which 1O2 played a small role in the generation of CL while •OH and •O2 – were the main intermediates in present CL system.

Possibility of energy transfer: the reaction mechanism of oxidation sulphite with KMnO4/Ce(IV) was discussed in detail by Jaeschke and Stauff [31], in which excited state SO2 molecules (SO2*) were the carrier of the CL (emitting radiation in the range of 300 to 450 nm wavelengths), and could transferred their energy to some fluorescent compounds which acted as sensitizers in enhanced CL reaction system [3]. Similarly, SO2* from the reaction of K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 could transferred its energy to MFCD through SO2 moleculars with MFCD nanoparticles collision as well as the emission spectrum range of SO2* overlapped with the excitation wavelength of the MFCD, and the QY of MFCD was higher than that of SO2*, ultimately, a stronger CL signal occurred.

In view of the above discussions, the possible mechanism of the CL reaction could be best expressed as following: firstly, •HSO3 was formed from the reaction of HSO3 – and Cr2O7 2– under acidic conditions; then the recombination of •HSO3 anionic radicals could generate an intermediate as SO2*, which was unstable and decomposes to SO2, releasing energy to emit a weak light; the excited SO2* maybe transferred its energy to MFCD through energy transfer process to form excited MFCD*, which returned to ground state following stronger CL [32,33]. Besides, the produced •OH and •O2 – radicals could reacted with MFCD by hole-electron injection generating MFCD•+ and MFCD•–, which lead to MFCD* species accompanied by CL emission. The mechanism stated above can be illustrated as in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CL system for the determination of iodide
ce:section.ce:para: As it was described, iodide enhanced the CL intensity of the MFCD-NaHSO3-K2Cr2O7 system (Fig. 4a,c). The redox reaction between K2Cr2O7 and iodide in acidic medium was accompanied by the release of energy or the generation of intermediates, the released energy was absorbed by MFCD or the intermediates may reacted with MFCD, both of which promoted the formation of MFCDs*, so the CL intensity of the MFCD-NaHSO3-K2Cr2O7 system in the presence of iodide was increased. This CL system was developed as a highly sensitive FI analysis for the determination of iodide in real samples. In order to obtain the highest sensitivity, the effects of the concentration of MFCD, K2Cr2O7, NaHSO3, and the type and concentration of acids on the response of iodide (ΔI = I − I 0, defined as the difference between the CL intensity of this system with (I) and without (I 0) iodide) were investigated. As can be seen from the results in Fig. S3, the best response for iodide was obtained by 1/50 (v/v) MFCD, 0.5 mM K2Cr2O7, 7.0 mM NaHSO3, and 0.4 M H2SO4.

Under these optimized conditions, the CL emission intensity of the MFCD-NaHSO3-K2Cr2O7 system in the presence of iodide with different concentrations was monitored and the calibration graph was plotted as the ΔI values versus the logarithm of iodide concentration (Fig. 4d). The linear range was in the concentration range of 0.03–5.0 μM with a regression equation ΔI = 7104 + 4048 lg[I–] (r = 0.9954). The detection limit (LOD) was to be 5.6 nM, which was calculated by 3s b/S, S showed the calibration slope, and s b was the standard deviation of CL signals for eleven repetitive determination of blank solutions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for eleven repeated measurements of 0.4 μM iodide was 1.42%. Compared with the some previously reported methods for the measurement of iodide, mainly FL [16−18,34,35] or CL-based systems [19,20,36], the results in Table 1 revealed that the LOD of proposed method was comparable to, and even lower than those, which showed a relatively great sensitivity towards iodide.

For the real samples, some substances may cause potential interference for the experimental system, therefore, the study of selectivity in the determination of iodide was done before its application to real samples. The tolerable limit of a foreign species was considered as a relative error less than ±5% in CL signal at a concentration of 0.4 μM iodide (Table 2 ). The results showed that only Hg2+ and Ag+ had a certain degree of influence. In order to eliminate the possible interference from Hg2+ and Ag+ ions, the samples were treated by adding sulfide, and sodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA) was added to the samples as a masking agent of metal ions. Other substances yielded a negligible influence on the detection of iodide, illustrating that the system used to detect iodide had a good selectivity.

The CL assay was applied to detect iodide in mineral water and milk. To evaluate the accuracy of the method, recovery experiments were performed, and the results were illustrated in Table 3 . It was worth noting that recoveries for detection of iodide in above real samples ranged from 97.0% to 103.7%. The obtained results indicated that the reliability of the present method for iodide determination in real samples.

In summary, MFCD was found to be effectively enhance the sensitivity of the CL reaction between K2Cr2O7 and NaHSO3. In this CL system, MFCD could involve in the CL reaction as an energy acceptor and luminophor. Based on which, a new CL signal amplification platform was established. When iodide was added into the MFCD-K2Cr2O7-NaHSO3 system, it could strongly interact with K2Cr2O7 to release more energy and exhibit amplifying effect on the CL system. Thus, a sensitive probe based on the MFCD-enhanced CL system was developed for the measurement of iodide and its usefulness was successfully tested by measuring iodide in real samples. Noteworthy that this work could provide a new direction to construct novel CL systems and will push forward for more efficient application in food detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and that there has been no financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (no. 2013011013–3), and the 1331 Engineering of Shanxi Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104638.
